data.title: SFL Corporation: Weighing The Fallout Of A Dividend Cut And An Idle Hercules Rig
data.description:
Thesis

SFL Corp. (SFL) recently cut its quarterly dividend by 26% to $0.20/share. The company’s decision to cut the dividend was driven by the continued unemployment of the Hercules vessel and reduced revenues following the disposition of 21 container and dry bulk vessels.

Following the significant shakeup in the company’s fleet, SFL’s revenues have certainly gotten a makeover. However, despite the changes, the outlook for the idle Hercules rig continues to be the dominant factor for the company’s financials.

With this rig still unemployed but remaining in a hot-stacked condition, the company is approaching a neutral free cash flow position even after the reduced dividend. The harsh environment drilling market is expected to improve in 2026, but this market also faces competition from other service providers.

Due to the high dependence on the success or failure of the Hercules vessel, I am rating SFL as a HOLD.

A Busted Growth Thesis

When I last covered SFL, in the summer of 2024, a lot of things were going right for the company. The company had just secured robust long-term contracts on five tanker vessels, and the Hercules was beginning a very lucrative contract in Canda.

However, as the calendar turned to 2025, things started to head south. In fact, the stock has registered a total return of over -30% since I last discussed the company, making it one of my worst recommendations here on SA. Although this is highly disappointing, the only thing to do at this point is pick up the pieces and try to take an objective view of where to go from here.

Hercules Remains The Biggest Story, But The Story Had A Plot Twist

By now it should be highly evident that the financial success of SFL rests with the Hercules rig. This vessel can command a significant contract rate in the range of $300,000-$500,000/d. As recently as Q4 2024, this vessel was contracted at the high end of this range, accounting for nearly 20% of the company’s revenue.

Fast forward to today, and this vessel has been idle for the vast majority of the year while it sits in a hot-stacked condition in search of its next contract. This situation keeps the vessel’s operating costs elevated while revenue generation has fallen to zero.

The company’s management is still in search of new work for this rig but holds out hope for promising activity levels developing for harsh environment semi-submersibles in 2026. However, the Hercules will be pitted against competing rigs from key competitors such as Transocean (RIG), Noble (NE), Seadrill (SDRL), and Valaris (VAL). These companies also have significant white space in their contract books for similar vessels, although overall competitiveness can vary greatly in this niche area of offshore drilling.

While in the hot stacked configuration, the company expects this vessel to log an operating cost of $80,000/d. The company has elected to absorb this additional cost as it sees this as the lesser of two evils compared to cold stacking the unit. SFL has also taken this downtime to further upgrade the rig and catch up on neglected maintenance that remains outstanding from the rig's unfortunate return from Seadrill.

Ideally, these efforts should ensure that the Hercules vessel is ready to go should the market call for its services. But that “should” remains the crux of the problem. 2025 has not been kind to oil prices and has caused many companies to pause and reassess their development plans.

The target market for the Hercules is in Norway, where the vessel is currently stacked. The current outlook has activity projected to pick up by mid-2026. However, it would not surprise me to see some of this activity get delayed now that OPEC+ has fully opened its doors by bringing 2.2 million barrels per day of projection into the market in just a few short months.

Despite the timing uncertainty, there will always be the notion of getting paid to wait until the Hercules rig picks up a contract. This would be playing the market for an eventual rebound, and that strategy has merit. On the other hand, I feel this strategy is probably not in line with the rest of SFL’s portfolio of steady long-term contracts. This may make this play unappealing to the yield-seeking investor.

Changes In Cash Flow After Trimming The Portfolio

Beyond the situation regarding the Hercules rig, SFL reported a significant alteration in its fleet composition in the second quarter earnings release. The company parted ways with 21 vessels, including 12 dry bulk carriers and 8 container ships. The majority of these vessels were late in life, allowing the company to recycle capital into renewing the fleet.

Disposing of these vessels clearly will impact revenues and operating expenses, so it’s worth taking a look at potential future cash flows. For a rough estimate, I have assumed that earnings for the dry bulk segment go to essentially zero now that the fleet has shrunk from 15 vessels to 3. I have also modeled in a 25% reduction in earnings from the container segment.

These estimates are rather crude but are at least reasonable and likely a bit conservative. In this way, I now expect the company to generate breakeven cash flows after paying both the dividend and capital investments into the fleet.

In the absence of additional revenue from the Hercules vessel, the company will need to find accretive deals to provide additional margin to positive free cash flow. At quarters end, the company had $156 million in cash on the balance sheet. In addition, the company received $150 million via the sale of 12 of the company’s dry bulk vessels. The combined liquidity should be substantive enough to broker a deal, but opportunities may be in short supply in the near term given the current volatility in the market.

Valuation

After accounting for the reduced revenue from the previously discussed vessel sales, I expect EBITDA to reduce to roughly $95 million moving forward. When annualized, this generates an EV:EBITDA value of roughly 9.5x. This is a significant step up from the trailing multiple of 7.6x.

Effectively, the company’s debt load dwarfs the market cap and cash balance. Because of this, a relatively small change in EBITDA has a notable change in the company’s valuation. In my view, SFL doesn’t warrant a premium multiple just because it has a lucrative yield. As noted in the free cash flow discussion, the dividend has minimal margin. This makes the investment somewhat at risk to future dividend cuts.

Risks

The direction of global oil prices will drive the overall timeline for the Hercules vessel. Based on OPEC+ production increases, global oil markets are in a state of surplus. Global demand growth will likely not catch up to these production increases until mid-to-late 2026.

Therefore, any economic distress or further downward pressure on oil prices could impact investment in new oil projects. The result for SFL could be longer idle times for this key asset. The longer this holds true, the longer the company sits in a somewhat precarious neutral free cash flow position.

Investor Key Takeaways

  1. The next window of opportunity for the Hercules rig is likely mid-2026, but this is subject to change based on the performance of global oil markets.
  2. The changes to the dividend payout and fleet structure put the company at a near-breakeven free cash flow profile.
  3. The resulting EBITDA reduction degrades the company’s valuation and is expensive for a company facing moderate operational challenges and a high debt load.
  4. The risk versus reward aspects of a position in SFL are oddly skewed between long-term contracts and the dependence on the Hercules vessel. Investors seeking stable income may be better suited to other securities.
  5. I rate SFL as a HOLD due to an expensive valuation and moving target on recontacting of the Hercules rig.

data.title: ACP: 15%+ Yield, Discount, But Long-Term Underperformance
data.description:
Will the Fed cut rates in September? If it does, existing fixed-income vehicles should get a boost. Of course, the market has been waiting for quite some time for a rate cut, with the hope that the cut(s) will benefit several industries and sectors.

The Abrdn Income Credit Strategies Fund (NYSE:ACP) yields over 15% and is trading at a discount to NAV. It's down slightly so far in 2025.

When we last covered ACP in January '25, we rated it a Sell due to reduced prospects for a rate cut in 2025. Even with increased potential for a September rate cut from the Fed, we maintain our Sell rating on ACP due to several reasons, which we'll discuss below.

Fund Profile

"ACP aims to seek a high level of current income with a secondary objective of capital appreciation. The Fund seeks to achieve its investment objectives by opportunistically investing primarily in debt and loan instruments of issues that operate in a variety of industries and geographic regions. The Fund may invest, without limitation, in credit obligations that are rated below investment grade.

The Fund's policy is to provide common shareholders with a stable monthly distribution out of current income, supplemented by realized capital gains and, to the extent necessary, paid-in capital, which is a non-taxable return of capital." (ACP site)

With $767M in Net Assets, ACP has 152 holdings and 525K in daily volume. Management uses leverage, 28% as of 6/30/25, which added 2.20% to its expense ratio of 4.44%. Its leverage is considerably higher than the 15% it had in early October '24. ACP began in January 2011.

Holdings

Consumer Discretionary continues to be ACP's highest allocation, followed by Materials, at 13.7%, which is up 110 basis points vs. 11/30/24. Financials have increased by 360 basis points, to 11.7%, while Consumer Staples have risen 260 basis points. Capital Goods have declined 170 basis points, and Energy has dropped 110 basis points since 11/30/24.

As of 6/30/25, 79% of ACP's portfolio was allocated to maturities of 0-5 years, down from 82.3% at 11/30/24, while 18% was in 5-10 year maturities, up from 14%.

Non-investment-grade securities dominate the portfolio, with BB-rated holdings at ~20%, B-rated at 60.7%, and CCC at 14.7%.

ACP's top 10 holdings are each just 2.3% or less of its total portfolio, with the top 10 accounting for just 19% of its holdings. There was quite a lot of turnover since 11/30/24, with 7 new positions in the top 10 holdings: Bracken Midco, 888 Acquisitions, Summer BC US Holdco, Staples, Sherwood Financing, Fiesta Purchaser, and Wolseley Group.

The US, at ~28%, and the UK, at ~26%, continue to be ACP's top 2 regional exposures, with the US down 350 basis points and the UK up 270 basis points since 11/30/24.

The US Dollar remains the top currency exposure, at 55.7%, followed by the EURO, at 29.6%, and the British Pound, at 14.7%.

Dividends

At $5.96, ACP yields 15/60%. It pays $0.0775 monthly and should go ex-dividend next on ~9/19/25, with a 9/30/25 pay date. It has paid $.0775 since December '24, when the monthly distribution was cut from $.10.

ACP has a 5-year dividend growth average of -3.84%, with cuts in 2020, 2021, and 2024.

The pace of NII picked up dramatically in the 6 months ending 4/30/25, hitting $32.56M—that's up ~62% vs. $39.65M in the full fiscal year ended 10/31/2024 on an annualized basis. Realized losses improved ~23% vs. the full fiscal year ended 10/31/2024, on an annualized basis. Unrealized losses, however, swung from a $58M gain in the full fiscal year ended 10/31/2024 to -$36M in the 6 months ended 4/30/25.

Distributions for the 6 months ended 4/30/25 were $61M, with NII only covering ~53%, vs. ~48% in the full fiscal year ended 10/31/2024. ACP's asset base shrank ~$74M in the 6 months ended 4/30/25, to $746M.

Taxes

Distributions for the full fiscal year ended 10/31/2024 were characterized as ~63% Return of Capital (ROC). ROC offers a tax deferral advantage, but it does decrease your tax basis, which will impact your tax bite if you sell your shares.

Performance

As of 7/31/25, ACP has outperformed the Morningstar US CEF Senior Loan category in price and NAV over the past 3- and 6-month periods and 1-year period. It also outperformed it on an NAV basis over the past 3-year period, but lagged on a price basis due to its decline in 2022.

Longer term, ACP has lagged the category over the past 5- and 10-year periods.

ACP has outperformed its category so far in 2025 on a price and NAV basis and also did so in 2023, 2020, 2019, and 2017.

Risks

Currency Risk: As ACP holds ~45% of its portfolio in foreign currencies, adverse changes in currency valuations could pressure its value. In 2025, the US dollar has fallen ~12% vs. the Euro and ~7% vs. the British Pound.

With the dollar being ACP's highest exposure, at ~56%, the Euro at 29.6%, and the British Pound at 14.7%, ACP is getting some hedging benefit vs. the dollar's decline.

Interest Rate Risk: Rising interest rates will pressure fixed income vehicles, as evidenced in ACP's 22.5% loss in 2022, a year of rising rates.

Valuations

Buying CEFs at deeper than historic discounts to NAV can be a useful strategy due to mean reversion. CEFs' daily valuations are calculated at the end of each trading day.

As of the 8/20/25 close, ACP was trading at a 2.93% discount to its NAV/share. While that's not as deep as its 1-year average discount of 3.75%, it's deeper than its 3-year 2.05% discount and its 5-year 0.64% discount. Note that ACP's NAV/share was $23.83 at its inception in 2011.

Parting Thoughts

While ACP's 2.9% discount is deeper than its 3- and 5-year average pricing, the decline in its NAV/share, its underperformance over the past 5- and 10-year periods, and its history of dividend cuts cause us to rate it a Sell.

If you're interested in other high-yield vehicles, we cover them every Friday and Sunday in our articles.

All tables furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: PayPal: Don't Let The Steep Selloff Go To Waste
data.description:
PayPal: Fed Chair Powell Signals A September Rate Cut

A much-needed (relief) rally for investors of PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL), kudos to the dovish posture undertaken by Fed Chair Jerome Powell in the clearest signal yet of a September interest rate cut. I think it's still too early for us to ascertain whether it could be just another relief rally before the momentum fizzle out, or a harbinger to a more decisive breakout to a new high. Why?

Well, it's not as if the market hasn't been anticipating a rate cut starting in September. As a matter of fact, investors had baked slightly below four cuts over the next 12 months, aligning with the bullish optimism that drove the market to a new recent peak.

Nevertheless, the same cannot really be said of PYPL investors, as the stock of the fintech leader continues to struggle for traction. While it has also received a welcomed bullish jolt as I pen this article, it remains far below its January 2025 highs, demonstrating the substantive lack of conviction from the market about its recovery prospects. Clearly, as the S&P 500 (SPX, SPY) and its financial sector peers (XLF) notched new recent heights, PayPal investors must be lamenting about just what it would take for the market to regain confidence in the fintech leader once again.

PayPal: Near-Term Uncertainties Vs. Long-Term Growth Optionalities

In my last PayPal update, I reminded investors that the company boasts a two-sided merchant and consumer platform. While the valuation de-rating of its growth premium was justified, the worst of its decline appears to be over, behooving me to take a more positive stance amidst its challenges.

Facing the twin threats of intense competition and a potentially more pervasive economic slowdown, PayPal arguably has its work cut out, as it navigates transformative changes in the fintech and digital payments marketplace.

Notably, I believe astute and observant investors have likely scrutinized the incursions into PayPal's higher-margin Branded segment. Its previous venture into the less profitable (but higher growth potential) Unbranded opportunities was designed as the primary response to rescue its growth premium. However, as PayPal's Q2 earnings release has unveiled, it has decided to reset the balance between volume and margins. In that aspect, management has made it clear that it will continue to shift away from lower-margin Unbranded volume growth opportunities to better secure its transaction margins. However, that has also affected its Unbranded volume growth, which delivered flat YoY growth in Q2, highlighting the immense challenges in that growth/volume reset.

With that in mind, I wouldn't fault the market for taking the safer route, as it contemplated management's caution on consumer spending, while also taking into account the ambiguities engendered by tariff-related impacts. Hence, I thought Powell's speech at the Jackson Hole symposium lent substantially more clarity into the policy inclinations based on their current data points. And, I believe it's increasingly clear that the balance of employment and inflation risks have skewed further to the downside, threatening the jobs outlook. In addition, Powell's address implies that it's not immediately clear that the tariffs could lead to an inflationary spiral, threatening the economic outlook beyond 2026. Hence, it does seem like we should see a more sustained bottom for PYPL stock even as it treads water toward the rest of 2025.

However, while economic risks threatening PayPal's core e-commerce and omnichannel growth vectors should be mitigated, the emerging threats from stablecoins should persist. Thanks to the clarity afforded by the passing of the GENIUS Act, PayPal has also been moving rapidly to participate in the stablecoin ecosystem, but it's still too nascent for us to assess whether it's enough to carve out a long-term dominance against well-established peers.

Legacy payments players such as Visa (V) are also priming to partake in what could be transformative changes in the digital payments ecosystem, which could also upend their business models. Hence, the long-term outlook for PayPal could still be murky, as it wrangles with the ambivalence obscuring more favorable forecasts over the next five to ten years, which could jeopardize its valuation upgrade/re-rating potential moving ahead.

PayPal: Margins Still Expected To Stay Solid

Nevertheless, while I don't have a firm grasp on whether PayPal could circumvent these intense headwinds successfully, I also think it's crucial for us to give credence to its fundamentally strong platform.

Note that while the Unbranded payments have been somewhat deprioritized in favor of margin accretion, it should help the fintech company pave the way for a medium-term margin recovery.

And I believe it's noteworthy that, in spite of the current challenges afflicting its revenue growth and transaction margins trajectory, it's still expected to be substantially free cash flow profitable.

Consequently, it has afforded PYPL to continue supporting aggressive share repurchases (current annualized run rate: $6B). I acknowledge that the growth optionalities in stablecoin, crypto, Agentic Commerce, and the newly launched PayPal World integration are considered nascent and might not materially move the needle in the short term. Yet, I believe we need to give management more time to overcome the near-term hurdles of resetting its volume growth strategy, while executing the go-to-market in these new vectors, and also fending off threats from opportunistic big tech and platform companies keen on encroaching further into PYPL's turf.

Buy Or Sell PYPL Stock?

And also, I can truly comprehend the disappointment experienced by PYPL investors, as the stock's underperformance against XLF since the start of 2025 doesn't seem to augur well. PayPal is, without a doubt, facing monumental challenges aiming to upend its platform moat, as volume growth stalled.

Hence, with the introduction of more well-endowed stablecoin competitors seeking to overturn the advantages enjoyed by the legacy players (and that includes PYPL), it has arguably compounded the pre-existing headwinds that have afflicted its growth prospects.

Yet, I have also observed that PYPL/XLF has still managed to hold the current defense line, suggesting all isn't lost yet. Interestingly, PYPL's underperformance against XLF hasn't collapsed to a new nadir, suggesting that accumulation at the current levels seems apt.

And, with the stock printing a forward PEG ratio of 1.32 (aligned closely with the financial median of 1.26), the premium that was previously accorded to the stock is arguably long gone. Hence, I assess that patient investors with a longer-term horizon to wait out the current consolidation might find the current setup appealing to buy more shares.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: NIO Stock Jumps: New Model Changes Everything
data.description:
My Thesis Update

It's been a while since I covered NIO Inc. (NIO) stock last time, but it looks like my upgrade in April of this year has been playing out quite well for the past few weeks:

What's been driving the stock price to the upside was NIO's continued operational shift that I was pointing to last time, and as a key part of that shift, we saw the news that the firm unveiled its new model [ES8], which is cheaper versus their previous models, and it's considered to compete with others in the Chinese car market. I think this is exactly what we can call a game-changer for NIO's prospective operational growth because the high price marks of its past models were the main explanation for the business expansion in the past. Now it's likely to change, and NIO seems to have the needed resources to make it happen. I'm reiterating my "Buy," waiting for new highs ahead.

My Updated Reasoning

For Q1 2025, NIO delivered 42,094 vehicles, which was a 40.1% YoY increase, leading to sales of ~$1.67 billion (+21.5% YoY). The market was expecting more, and the same thing is true regarding NIO's bottom-line improvements (the firm missed the adjusted EPS estimate by ~5 cents):

It's hard to say that analysts and general market participants were expecting a strong beat from NIO because, statistically speaking, it would have been a clear outlier to what everyone's already got used to.

Although the consensus systematically fails to assign close-to-reality expectations, I think that NIO did deliver some good news in its Q1 release. For example, I liked the fact that their vehicle margin improved to 10.2% from 9.2% in Q1 2024, and it made the gross profit margin soar up to 7.6% (from 4.9% last year), so the key margin almost doubled on a YoY basis, which was great to see. Having excluded Lucid Auto (LCID) from the peer group because its COGS exceeds revenues, I see that NIO's GP trend is generally in sync with what other Chinese automakers show.

As you can see, the lag with the peers is still quite high, and NIO's GP margin should at least triple to be comparable to those of BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF)(OTCPK:BYDDY) or Li Auto (LI). It's not an easy thing to do, and it explains the persistent underperformance of NIO's stock versus BYD and others.

But things started to change recently, with NIO coming out as a strong leader even despite the massive misses on the consensus estimates in recent quarters.

Why so?

I think the firm's strategic re-focus has started to work out better than many skeptics had been expecting before. First, the GP margin is already on the rise, and the strength of the rise looks promising in itself. Second, the company recently rolled out a new ES8 model, whose price is set "lower than the starting price for the Tesla (TSLA) Model Y L six-seat SUV" (at ~$58K, which is going to be a 25% reduction from the previous generation).

The redesigned ES8 expands from a mid-large to a full-size SUV to become the largest battery-electric SUV in China. Built on NIO's 12 full-stack technological capabilities, it was highlighted by the company as combining an expressive third-generation "Design for Success" philosophy with premium materials, bespoke interiors, and airplane-style zero-gravity seating configurations.

Source: Seeking Alpha News

That was a very important game-changer because a lower-priced full-size SUV is exactly what NIO needed to broaden its product line and re-accelerate its delivery growth in the next few years. Luxury SUVs and SUVs in general are one of the highest-growth auto markets that gain market share within the auto mix constantly.

It's great for NIO to be there and offer an optimally priced vehicle to its customers. It's adding more chances for NIO to get a much higher gross profit margin as they keep scaling on this new front, especially taking into account that over the past few years, the firm gained enough technical expertise to develop some of the needed chips in-house (that's a major cost savings versus Nvidia Orin X). And they can also save up on R&Ds thanks to R&D cost sharing across multiple models with their 900V platform.

William Li explained how the cost was saved to achieve such a cost reduction, mentioning a few main aspects. Nio renegotiated the cost with suppliers, lowering the procurement cost. R&D cost was spread across multiple models, sharing the core technologies such as the 900 V charging platform, rear motor, and in-house developed chip. The ES8 also underwent some material adjustments with a reduction of aluminium content.

Source: From CarNewsChina [August 22, 2025]

I'd expect something close to at least 15-17% cost savings for the upcoming Q2 (a bit higher at the midpoint vs. the management's guidance), and it's going to help NIO return to double-digit gross profit margin in Q2 (and hopefully beyond that). NIO can break even on EBITDA, assuming a) monthly deliveries of over 50,000 units, b) vehicle gross margins of 17-18%, and c) well-thought-out OPEX/CAPEX management.

And going into the fourth quarter, we are going to introduce our all new ES8, the next generation ES8, which is a highly competitive product. With that, we believe that in Q4, for the NIO brand, the monthly delivery will be around 25,000 units. That's around 20% year-over-year growth from last year's Q4, which was around 20,000 units a month. In that case, while improving the sales volume, we are also going to improve our vehicle gross margin significantly for the NIO brand to be above 20% in Q4.

Source: From NIO's Q1 FY2025 earnings call

It's too early to tell about the EPS (even on an adjusted basis), but with the recent events in mind, I'm not so sure that the current EPS breakeven on FY2028 is correctly reflecting the real odds. I think NIO can now make it a bit faster.

At 1-1.2x of forward sales, NIO's market cap should be $16.2-19.44 billion for me to call it fairly valued (again, with the core assumption that NIO's profitability prospects are now closer to reality than ever before).

This gives us an upside of 13.7-36.5% to the current stock price, even after Friday's surge of over 14%.

Risks And Your Takeaway

I admit some fragility to my bullish thesis on NIO because if its new ES8 model doesn't gain as much popularity as, for example, Li Auto's Lixiang model range did in the past few years, the firm's operational struggles are likely to come back to their past questionable conditions, not to mention the market narrative around the stock. All investors should keep in mind that NIO's plans to break even can be shifted again and again, and the firm will likely need a few rounds of external capital injections (hence dilutions) to finance its strategic plans. Yes, they hold over $2.7 billion in cash on the balance sheet, but their TTM-based EBIT is negative ~$3.15 billion, so another share sale is a question of "when", not "if."

Anyway, NIO is still a high-growth stock, and it has just confirmed that with both the ES8 roll-out (high growth in future sales/earnings) and a massive bullish reaction in terms of the stock price action. I think that reaction is there to stay with us for some time: Assuming the stock can reprice to reasonable levels on the P/S side, the stock price can gain another 14-37% in a matter of weeks, if not days. And if the company starts to show higher GP margins, as I expect, the stock price upside will be much higher than that.

I decided to keep my "Buy" on NIO in active mode for now.

Good luck with your investments!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Whirlpool: We Maintain Our Buy Rating Despite The Macroeconomic Headwinds
data.description:
Whirlpool (NYSE:WHR) is one of the most well-known household brands in the world. They are famous for their home appliances and related products, which they manufacture and sell internationally.

We started covering the company back in 2022 and have published 5 articles since then. Initially we had a neutral view on the firm, but later we came more bullish due to the attractive valuation based on dividend discount models and improving fundamentals.

Today our goal is to give an updated view on WHR's business, taking into account the changes in the macroeconomic environment, the firm's valuation, as well as the latest quarterly results, published late July.

Macroeconomic environment and quarterly results

The current macroeconomic environment and its direction do not appear to be positive for WHR's business.

Consumer confidence

First, let us take a look at consumer confidence in the United States. The following chart depicts how consumer sentiment has changed over the past decade.

Consumer confidence is a leading economic indicator, which is often used to gauge how consumer spending is likely to change in the near future. Poor sentiment often means that people are less certain about their financial situation and less likely to spend on durable discretionary goods, like the ones WHR is selling.

The low consumer confidence is already visible in WHR's latest earnings. Due to the low demand globally, net sales decreased by 5.4% year-over-year.

These headwinds are expected to last for the rest of 2025 at least. And they are clearly reflected in both the management's guidance and in analyst estimates.

Guidance figures were substantially revised to the lower side in the previous quarter for each segment:

The cash flow guidance was also revised lower, although we are still expecting minimal growth compared to the prior year.

Analysts also see WHR's sales falling for the coming years, along with their earnings.

Last, but not least, we would also like to point out that the low demand, combined with increasing inventory, leads to a decreasing inventory turnover, which may potentially lead to obsolete inventory and eventually to lower profitability in the future.

Tariffs

Tariffs were in the spotlight for most of the year and investors were having difficulties gauging how the constantly changing policies might influence the players in the industry and their financial performance.

WHR seems to be well-positioned to withstand the negative impacts of the tariffs, as 80% of their production is handled by domestic U.S. players. In contrast, WHR's competitors are largely importers, importing mainly from China and Mexico.

Home sales

In the firm's latest quarterly report, we can also read about the correlation between the demand for WHR's products and existing home sales. The following slide shows that existing home sales are below historical averages, driven primarily by the rapid interest rate increases. The company does not expect the demand for existing homes to pick up significantly in the near term; therefore, they also expect lower demand for their products in the coming months and quarters.

Interest rate

Interest rate is a significant driver of both consumer confidence and the demand for homes and discretionary products. The Fed increased interest rates rapidly in 2022 as a response to growing inflation.

Although the peak is likely behind us, the Fed is not likely to cut rates aggressively. Inflation in the recent months increased again, which is likely leading to an even lower likelihood of a near term rate cut.

All in all, we believe that the current macroeconomic environment is creating significant headwinds for not only WHR's business, but for the whole industry. These headwinds are not likely to ease in the near term.

Valuation

To assess WHR's fair value, we are going to look at a set of traditional price multiples today. The following summarizes these multiples:

Based on these metrics, we can see that WHR's stock appears to be trading at a significant discount compared to both the consumer discretionary sector median, and its own historic valuation metrics.

On one hand, we understand the reason for the discount, as highlighted above, poor consumer sentiment, a struggling housing market, the uncertainty around upcoming interest rate cuts are all creating headwinds for WHR in the near term. On the other hand, the current valuation is difficult to ignore for long-term investors. If someone is ready to stick with WHR in the long run, we believe that eventually decreasing interest rates are likely to boost both consumer confidence and the housing market, leading to higher demand for WHR's products. As it seems now the firm is also likely to benefit from the U.S. tariff policies. Last, but not least we need to mention that WHR is also a responsible dividend payer, paying $0.9 quarterly, which corresponds to an annual yield of roughly 4.1% currently. Although it is a significant step back from the amount paid in the previous years - $1.75 quarterly, we understand the rationale behind the dividend cut, and agree with this capital allocation strategy.

All in all, we believe that WHR is attractive from a valuation point of view at the current price levels.

Conclusions

The macroeconomic environment is currently causing significant headwinds to WHR's business. Poor consumer confidence, low existing home sales and high interest rates are all negatively impacting the demand for WHR's products and therefore the firm's financial performance.

The last quarterly results also show sales decline, and the 2025 guidance was also revised lower across all metrics.

On the other hand, WHR may benefit from the tariff policies as roughly 80% of their production is done domestically.

From a valuation point of view, WHR's stock appears appealing. The stock is selling at a discount compared to both the sector median and the firm's own historic metrics. We believe that the macroeconomic factors are already priced in.

For these reasons, we believe that WHR could be an attractive stock to buy at the current valuation for investors, who have a longer-term focus.

data.title: Why I Stopped Buying Rental Properties To Buy REITs Instead
data.description:
After witnessing the poor recent performance of REITs (VNQ), I wouldn't blame you for thinking that they are worse investments than rental properties.

They have been in a bear market for the past 3+ years, and have lost over 20% of their value on average:

Even then, I still continue to believe that REITs are far better investments than rental properties, especially today.

In short, they offer much stronger risk-and-hassle-adjusted returns over the long run in most cases, and that is why I switched from private real estate to REIT investing, and I ain't going back.

Below, I am going to explain all of that to you in three separate sections:

  • Why REITs Earn Higher Returns Than Rental Properties
  • Why REITs Are A Lot Safer Than Rental Properties
  • How REITs Optimize Your Career, Lifestyle, and Freedom

Why REITs Earn Higher Returns Than Rental Properties

Studies show that REITs earn 2-4% higher annual returns than private real estate on average over the long run.

Here are three of those studies:

This surprises many investors who believe that private real estate must be more rewarding thanks to its leverage, tax benefits, and lack of management costs.

But those are all just misconceptions.

First, REIT investors also enjoy the benefits of leverage. The difference is that investors do not need to take out a mortgage themselves. It is the REIT that handles all of that. When you are buying shares, you are investing in the equity of the REIT, on top of which, most REITs add leverage to boost your returns.

Second, REITs are incredibly tax-efficient as well. They pay no corporate tax, retain a portion of their cash flow that's tax-deferred, classify some of their distribution as return of capital, and the dividend also enjoys a 20% deduction.

Finally, despite paying millions to their top executives, REIT management costs as a percentage of assets are typically far lower than those of private real estate, thanks to the massive economies of scale they enjoy.

Here are eight reasons why REITs outperform private real estate in most cases:

  • Reason #1: They enjoy significant economies of scale, not just in their management, but in all other cost items ranging from property maintenance to brokerage, legal, and everything in between. Real estate is a low-margin business, so this goes a long way.
  • Reason #2: REITs can grow faster thanks to spread investing. As long as they can raise capital in the public market at a cost that's inferior to the expected returns of their investments, they can create value for shareholders.
  • Reason #3: REITs will often develop their own properties, earning higher returns than you could by buying stabilized assets.
  • Reason #4: REITs will also do sale and leaseback transactions, skipping brokerage fees, and allowing them to buy portfolios of properties with a bulk discount and better lease terms.
  • Reason #5: REITs will often earn additional profits by offering services to other investors, benefiting their shareholders.
  • Reason #6: Most REITs specialize in rapidly growing property sectors like data centers, e-commerce warehouses, and cell towers, which generally earn higher returns than single-family rentals.
  • Reason #7: REITs enjoy stronger bargaining power with their tenants because they are better diversified and have much better resources and relationships to release the space if needed. This allows REITs to be more aggressive with rent hikes.
  • Reason #8: REITs can afford to hire the best talent in the real estate sector, which ultimately allows them to find the best deals and earn higher returns.

With that in mind, it makes sense that REITs would outperform private real estate investments in most cases. They enjoy many structural advantages that small landlords simply cannot compete with.

And I think that the outperformance of REITs will only be greater in the coming years because they are currently trading at historically low valuations, with many being offered at large discounts to their net asset values.

BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA), Camden Property Trust (CPT), Kite Realty Group (KRG), etc., all trade at about 30% discounts to their net asset values.

Historically, whenever REITs have been this cheap relative to private real estate, they have massively outperformed in the following years:

I don't think that this time will be different. As interest rates are cut in the coming years, we expect REITs to strongly recover, and this is why I am so bullish on them today.

Why REITs Are A Lot Safer Than Rental Properties

Typically, if you want to earn higher returns, you need to take higher risks.

But REITs are not only more rewarding, but they are also safer investments.

Here are eight reasons why:

Reason #1: REITs are liquid, making it much easier and cheaper for investors to buy or sell their investment.

Reason #2: REITs are large and diversified, which greatly reduces risks when compared to concentrated private landlords.

Reason #3: REITs use less leverage in most cases. The average LTV in the REIT sector is just 35% compared to closer to 60% for private real estate.

Reason #4: REIT investors enjoy true limited liability, as minority shareholders of a publicly listed company.

Reason #5: REITs are professionally managed by the best talent in real estate, which will naturally do a better job at picking safer properties and managing them in a prudent way. They also keep up with the latest norms and regulations.

Reason #6: REITs detach you completely from social risks. When you are a landlord, you have people put their lives in your assets, which can lead to lots of social issues. With REITs, you are completely detached from them.

REITs are public, liquid, diversified, SEC-regulated, professionally managed, and provide true limited liability.

Rental properties are private, illiquid, concentrated, highly leveraged, and untransparent investments with significant liability risk.

The risk profile is night and day.

How REITs Optimize Your Career and Lifestyle

Finally, another major reason why I think that REITs will lead to greater wealth over time is that they allow you to fully focus on your career.

If you own rental properties, this will be a distraction that will take away from time and energy, and it will also tie you down to a specific location, limiting your geographic flexibility. This will slow down your career progression.

With REITs, you can fully focus on your career, which will allow you to get that next pay raise a lot faster. This matters a lot more than earning an extra percentage or two on your rental.

Beyond that, REITs will also give you a lot more freedom to optimize your lifestyle, which will ultimately give you a greater sense of happiness. You won't be stressed out by your tenants, your mortgages, roof leaks, etc. With REITs, you can worry about what truly matters and have the freedom to do whatever you like.

Closing Note

Yes, REITs have performed poorly over the past 3 years, but that was a very unusual period, coming off the pandemic and suffering a historic surge in inflation and interest rates.

Over the long run, REITs have been fantastic investments, outperforming even the S&P500 over the past 50-year time period.

We are happy to buy the dips at High Yield Landlord and grow our portfolio, while REITs remain so heavily discounted.

data.title: Goodbye Growth? Here's What I'm Buying As Value Mounts A Comeback
data.description:
Capital investment from the technology sector has not been this high since 2000. So far, this has been a mostly circular flow of spending within the tech space in a symbiotic relationship between hardware and software players.

That makes it concerning to learn of a recent study finding zero return on investment from the vast majority of organizations implementing artificial intelligence tools.

For personal use, we all know how helpful and productivity-enhancing AI can be. But on an enterprise basis, successful integration into workflows has apparently been harder than one would think.

That raises an ominous set of questions: Aren't enterprises supposed to be the primary customer for the legion of emerging AI tools coming to market? And if enterprise adoption of AI happens slower and more gradually than the market thinks, who is ultimately paying for all this AI investment?

These hanging questions go a long way in explaining techy-growth stocks' slide last week. (Last minute addition: Fed chairman Jay Powell's Jackson Hole speech seems to have swept all this concern about enterprise AI implementation under the rug, for now.)

And as Big Tech retrenched a bit, value-oriented dividend stocks have shone, proving their defensiveness and resilience.

Is this a blip or the start of a new trend? Only time will tell.

Here's the agenda for this week:

  • Diving into a troubling MIT study and exploring how the use of AI, however profitable, has already impacted the labor market.
  • A reminder of just how richly valued and complacent the market, especially some segments of it, appears to be right now. Credit spreads are ultra-tight, and a huge percentage of stock buybacks are non-accretive. Could value's rebound have already begun?
  • This dividend growth investor's three capital allocation priorities right now.

Onward!

The AI Profit Mirage?

A recent MIT study into the profitability impact of AI usage found that, among hundreds of organizations that collectively spent $30-40 billion on AI implementation, a whopping 95% of users saw zero returns on their investment.

To quote a Fortune article on the report:

Despite the rush to integrate powerful new models, about 5% of AI pilot programs achieve rapid revenue acceleration; the vast majority stall, delivering little to no measurable impact on P&L. The research—based on 150 interviews with leaders, a survey of 350 employees, and an analysis of 300 public AI deployments—paints a clear divide between success stories and stalled projects.

This isn't to say that companies won't keep trying to integrate AI tools, nor that these AI tools won't have a significant impact on productivity and labor markets over time.

In fact, there are already signs of labor market impacts. Quoting Fortune again:

Workforce disruption is already underway, especially in customer support and administrative roles. Rather than mass layoffs, companies are increasingly not backfilling positions as they become vacant. Most changes are concentrated in jobs previously outsourced due to their perceived low value.

This goes a long way in explaining why the unemployment rate remains so low despite weak job growth in recent months. As Baby Boomers retire and leave the labor force (especially those in administrative or "back office" roles), companies simply aren't hiring anyone to replace them. Thus, the labor force participation rate has dropped even as the unemployment rate has stayed rangebound.

If the labor force participation rate had stayed rangebound as well (that is, if job leavers went on to look for another job rather than retiring), then the unemployment rate would have surged to almost 5% at this point.

Clearly, AI is replacing some human labor by making workers more efficient. But that does not necessarily mean that paid versions of AI tools that enterprises are adopting and pushing on their workers are generating strong returns on investment.

It may simply indicate that the free versions of AI tools like ChatGPT or Gemini are being used in individualized ways by workers to make themselves more productive.

In other words, most of the productivity benefits from AI appear to come from free or low-cost chatbots and tools that workers use on an individualized basis, not the expensive, specialized, embedded applications that enterprises are trying to integrate into team workflows.

But if that is the case, and if enterprises pull back on AI application investment accordingly, then who will ultimately fund all of the capex being done in this space?

In Q2 2025, tech capex contributed about half a point to real GDP growth, about as much as consumer spending.

To my admittedly techno-illiterate eye, this MIT study looks like bad news for the biggest spenders on AI infrastructure and software development.

It does not mean that AI is all hype, but it may mean that commercial uses of AI at the enterprise level could be more limited and gradually implemented than is currently priced into tech stocks.

Market Maximalism and Value's Vengeance

When it comes to stock price performance, it's all about expectations versus reality. Sometimes real results are unimpressive, but expectations weren't that high either, and the stock price is therefore unaffected.

But in our current situation, expectations appear to be sky-high, which means that real results (especially for AI-related tech stocks) need to be extraordinarily good to justify expectations. So far, that seems to be the case. But so far, most tech companies' AI profits have come from the aforementioned circular flow of spending within the tech space.

Note that the forward price-to-earnings ratio of the S&P 500 (SPY) has been at or above its current level only twice in the last three decades: During the Dot Com bubble of the late 1990s and during the euphoric stimulus-fueled rally of 2020-2021.

In both cases, reality could not live up to expectations. In both cases, valuations eventually declined as the market dialed back its optimism from nosebleed levels.

If the reality of commercial applications of AI does not live up to its high expectations, it seems likely that the same pattern will play out this time as well.

Take one obvious example: Palantir Technologies (PLTR), the software developer that primarily works with the US government in various capacities. The company's growth runway is undoubtedly deep and robust, and it boasts the leading market position in its space.

And yet, the valuation appears to defy all logic or grounding in reality. PLTR's price to sales ratio jumped over the last few years from the teens to over 100x recently.

The P/E ratio currently sits above 240x.

Now, I don't doubt that PLTR is a highly valuable company doing highly valuable work. But to my (again, admittedly techno-illiterate) eye, it appears as though momentum has carried the valuation of this great company up and up into the heavenly realm of absurdities.

If stock valuations were inadequately pricing in strong fundamentals, you would expect to see company insiders tacitly signaling that by increasing their open-market purchases of company stock.

We are not seeing that today. In fact, insider buying is around the lowest it typically gets right now.

Of course, if stocks are fairly or excessively valued, this is exactly what you would expect to see from insiders.

Instead of insiders using their own cash for stock purchases, they are largely instead using company cash to do more and more buybacks. Perhaps unsurprisingly, this year is expected to see a new record for US stock buybacks.

The interesting thing is that buybacks are overwhelmingly concentrated among the biggest and often most richly valued companies on the market.

Take, for example, a company that shifted from being against buybacks to becoming the poster child for regular buybacks: Apple (AAPL). Since 2013, AAPL has continuously repurchased stock, quarter after quarter, retiring over 40% of shares outstanding in the last 12 years.

When AAPL began its buyback program, the stock's trailing twelve month P/E ratio hung around 10x. But as AAPL's P/E ratio has gone higher and higher over the last decade, the buybacks have continued, slowing only slightly.

Perhaps it made sense for AAPL to do buybacks at 10x earnings, but does it make sense to keep doing buybacks at today's ~30x forward P/E ratio?

Miller/Howard Investments recently put out a research report on S&P 500 buybacks versus dividends. Strikingly, they found that the share of non-accretive buybacks (that do not increase EPS) has surged in the last few years to its highest level on record — higher even than the Dot Com bubble era of the late 1990s.

If done at a sufficiently high valuation, stock buybacks cease to be accretive. As a share of market capitalization, non-accretive buybacks are at record levels right now.

Here is one more sign of market exuberance: credit spreads between high-yield/junk bonds and their US government equivalents. When the spread is wide, it indicates market panic and weak performance. Junk bond defaults spike, which decreases their appeal to investors. When the spread is narrow, it indicates market complacency and strong corporate bond performance.

Right now, credit spreads are at their tightest level since 2007.

I wouldn't necessarily interpret this as a sign of an impending market crash or recession, but it does tend to happen during the late stage of economic cycles.

All of the above makes me wonder if we are on or near the precipice of a major market rotation from growth to value. Of course, there have been many false starts in value's comeback against growth over the last decade and a half, so it would be fair to say that the long-running techy-growth outperformance over value is probably not over.

Even so, it's interesting to note that since the release of the MIT study on commercial AI profitability on August 18th, value-oriented stocks such as those in the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV) have outperformed techy-growth names such as those in the Nasdaq Index (QQQ) and Roundhill Generative AI & Technology ETF (CHAT):

Then on Friday, Jay Powell's dovish speech at Jackson Hole acted as a rising tide that lifted all boats in the stock market.

We'll have to watch and see if value's comeback is sustainable or merely a blip.

What I'm Buying Today

Powell's dovish Jackson Hole speech triggered a market-wide rally that took several individual stocks off my buy list, but there are still a handful of compelling opportunities among REITs.

My capital allocation priorities this week are as follows (from highest to lowest):

  1. Top up my positions in the individual REITs on my buy list.
  2. Build positions in the Brookfield baby bonds below.
  3. Resume building up my cash position.
Securities Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
Brookfield BRP Holdings 4.625% Sub Notes (BEPH) 7.0% N/A
BIP Bermuda Holdings I 5.125% Sub Notes (BIPI) 7.2% N/A
Brookfield Finance I Plc 4.5% Sub Notes (BNJ) 7.1% N/A
EastGroup Properties (EGP) 3.3% High-Single-Digit
Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS) 3.4% Mid-Single-Digit
Iron Mountain (IRM) 3.3% High-Single-Digit to Low-Double-Digit
Cash ETF
iShares Ultra Short Duration Bond Active ETF (ICSH) 4.5% N/A

The REITs

The Fed's signaling of imminent rate cuts may be just what the doctor ordered to rejuvenate real estate investment trusts ("REITs").

Note that after the MIT study's release on August 18th and especially after Powell's Jackson Hole speech on August 22nd, the real estate sector (VNQ) has been rebounding with vigor relative to the SPY:

REITs typically have very little floating rate debt, and thus Fed rate cuts have little direct impact on most REITs' balance sheets.

However, there is a second-order impact of a lower Fed Funds Rate. Total assets in money market funds has surged over the last few years to around $7.2 trillion, having been drawn in by the inflation-beating 4%+ yields.

As the FFR drops over the next year or so, income-generating equities like REITs will become increasingly attractive relative to money market funds and short-term bonds. Fund flows are likely to incrementally shift from these safer income sources to REITs and other higher-yielding stocks.

If the FFR drops significantly enough, then there may eventually be some fund outflows from money market funds and into REITs and other dividend stocks, but for the foreseeable future, the shifting flows are more likely to be incremental.

But even an incremental shift could be hugely bullish for REITs. According to NAREIT, the total market capitalization of all US equity REITs was about $1.4 trillion in 2024. Compare that to the roughly $1 trillion in funds that have flowed into MMFs over the last year.

If even a fraction of fund flows into MMFs are diverted into REITs, then REITs should enjoy a robust bull market going forward.

As of Friday, August 22nd, that shift already appears to be underway, and I think the tailwind for dividend stocks should last for a year or more.

The defensiveness of EGP's last-mile industrial facilities in the Sunbelt, the recession-resistant and inflation-resistant nature of ELS's core manufactured housing portfolio, and IRM's strong growth pipeline of data centers continue to look appealing to me, and I hope to top up my positions in these REITs while I still can at reasonable prices.

The Brookfield Baby Bonds

The three subordinated notes securities listed in the table above are issues of various subsidiaries of Brookfield Asset Management (BAM), one of the largest global asset managers in the world.

Brookfield is a complex financial machine, which causes many investors to steer clear of its securities. For most investors, that is probably a reasonable conclusion. Perhaps I am blinded by hubris, but I do believe I understand these securities well enough to include them within my buyable universe.

Each of these securities are subordinated notes, meaning that they are senior to common and preferred stock but junior to secured debt, of which Brookfield's subsidiaries make liberal use.

So, while the massive investment grade status of Brookfield reduces risk, the perpetually large amount of secured debt that's senior to these securities increases risk.

The fact that these baby bonds sport coupon yields of 4.5% to 5.125% makes it highly unlikely that they will be called anytime soon.

BIPI is backed by Brookfield Infrastructure (BIP, BIPC) and pays interest in March, June, September, and December.

BEPH is backed by Brookfield Renewable (BEP, BEPC) and pays interest in January, April, July, and October.

BNJ is backed by Brookfield International Corporate Finance Limited, which is a subsidiary of Brookfield Corporation (BN), the parent company of the whole Brookfield ecosystem. BNJ pays interest in February, May, August, and November.

Thus, owning all three of these securities will result in interest payments every month of the year.

To be clear, the complexity of Brookfield's ecosystem is a risk, in my view. That is why I will keep these baby bond positions relatively small ones in my portfolio. But at 7%+ yields, they also make for attractive income sources.

The Cash Equivalent ETF

ICSH owns a variety of conservative, ultra-short-duration income assets such as corporate bonds, Treasuries, and certificates of deposit, and its expense ratio is quite low at 0.08%.

With both high-growth tech stocks and value-oriented dividend stocks rallying on the back of impending Fed rate cuts, allocating the incremental dollar to stocks is becoming less attractive to me.

In my reckoning, as market complacency and valuations rise, so too should my cash position.

As such, this week, I hope to have at least a little bit of incoming cash left over to put into ICSH.

data.title: 10 Best CEFs This Month: Average Yield Of Nearly 9% (August 2025)
data.description:
Note: We are changing the format of this monthly article from 5 Best CEFs to 10 Best CEFs. This will provide more flexibility to readers to choose the funds that suit their individual needs and risk tolerance. Also, the focus of the selections will not be on the current valuation so much, but on other attributes that reflect the best value for the long term.

===

Introduction:

The S&P500 has been making new all-time highs, almost on a regular basis. However, so much of the gains have been concentrated in the large technology and high-growth names. So, obviously, the closed-end funds that have exposure to equities of high-growth and technology names have done better than others. But at the same time, some other sectors have also performed well, such as Utility funds, MLPs, and some foreign funds such as Taiwan Fund (TWN) and Mexico Fund (MXF). Also, the renewed expectations of interest rate cuts, the funds that are likely to benefit from declining interest rates are in focus.

All that being said, it is hard to find anything that has both the quality and the low valuation, so buying the best of the income investments regularly in small quantities is the key. As always, investors have to tread carefully amid all the uncertainties.

Why CEFs?

The broader market, as represented by the S&P500, provides a dismal level of yield of less than 1.20%. Income investors and retirees can't survive on that level of income unless they have a very large pool of capital. Alternatively, one can find solid blue-chip individual dividend stocks that pay much higher dividends than the S&P 500. They can also provide market-matching growth on a longer-term basis. To identify such stocks, we also publish a monthly article on the '5 Best DGI stocks'. You can find the most recent one here.

However, with CEFs, we can take the income to the next level. That said, they generally come with higher risks (discussed later), so we believe that an allocation of 20% to 30% to a diversified CEF portfolio should be good enough for most income investors. In summary, these investments should be used primarily for income, not growth.

We publish this monthly article on CEFs (Closed-end funds) to provide our readers with a select list of 10 CEFs that, as a group, provide wide diversification in terms of underlying assets, solid long-term performance at least on a relative basis, and are trading at decent valuations. Above all, the income goal for the group is to provide a yield of at least 8% or higher.

This month, as a group, the selected 10 best funds are offering an average distribution rate of just under 9% (as of 08/15/2025). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had an excellent record over the long term and collectively returned 13.7% and 16.3% (annualized) in the last three and five years, respectively. The average discount (to NAV) is good, at nearly -8%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Why Is It Important To Have A Diversified CEF Portfolio?

We believe a well-diversified CEF portfolio should contain at least 10 CEFs, preferably from different asset classes. It's also advisable to build the portfolio over a period rather than invest in one lump sum. If you were to invest in one CEF every month for a year, you would have a well-diversified CEF portfolio by the year's end. What we provide here every month is a list of five probable candidates for further research. A CEF portfolio can be an important component of the overall portfolio strategy. One should preferably have a DGI portfolio as the foundation, and the CEF portfolio could be used to boost the income level to the desired level. How much should one allocate to CEFs? Each investor needs to answer this question himself/herself based on their personal situation and factors like the size of the portfolio, income needs, risk appetite, or risk tolerance.

Criteria to Shortlist:

Criteria

Brings down the number of funds to...

Reason for the Criteria

Baseline expense < 2.5% and Avg. Daily Volume > 10,000

Approx. 435 Funds

We do not want funds that charge excessive fees. Also, we want funds that have fair liquidity.

Market capitalization> 100 Million

Approx. 400 Funds

We do not want funds that are too small.

Track record/ History longer than five years (inception date 2016 or earlier)

Approx. 375 Funds

We want funds that have a reasonably long track record.

Discount/Premium < +7%

Approx. 350 Funds

We do not want to pay too high a premium; we want bigger discounts.

Distribution (dividend) Rate > 5%

Approx. 250-290 Funds

The current distribution (income) is reasonably high.

5-Year Annualized Return on NAV > 0% AND/ OR

3-Year Annualized Return on NAV >0%

Approx. 200-250 Funds

We want funds with a reasonably good past track record in maintaining their NAVs.

After we applied the above criteria this month, we are generally left with around 250 funds (plus or minus 5%) on our list. From here on, we apply our other criteria to narrow the list.

Note: All tables in this article have been created by the author (unless explicitly specified). Most of the data in this article are sourced from Cefconnect.com, Cefa.com, and Morningstar.com.

Narrowing Down To 50 Funds

To narrow down the number of funds to a more manageable count, we will shortlist ten funds based on each of the following criteria. Thereafter, we will apply certain qualitative criteria to each fund and rank them to select the top five.

At this stage, we also eliminate certain funds with substantially negative NAV returns for both three-year and five-year periods.

Seven Broad Criteria:

  • Excess discount/premium (explained below).
  • Distribution rate.
  • Return on NAV, last three years (medium-term).
  • Return on NAV, last five years (long term).
  • Coverage ratio.
  • Excess return over distributions.
  • The total weight (calculated up to this point).

Excess Discount/Premium:

We certainly like funds that are offering large discounts (not premiums) to their NAVs. But what's important is to look at the "excess discount/premium" and not the absolute value. We want to see the discount (or premium) relative to their past record, say, a 52-week average.

Excess Discount/Premium = Current Discount/Premium (Minus) 52-Wk Avg. Discount/ Premium.

For more explanation of the excess discount and z-scores, please refer to our blog post (link provided at the beginning of the article).

We sort our list (of approx. 250 funds) on the "excess discount/premium" in descending order. For this criterion, the lower the value, the better it is. So, we select the top 10 funds (with the most negative values) from this sorted list.

(All data as of 08/15/2025)

Table 1:

High Current Distribution Rate:

After all, most investors invest in CEF funds for their juicy distributions. Even though we should not chase yield, at this stage, we want to give them a fair representation in our list and subject them to a more rigorous criterion later on. We sorted our list on the current distribution rate (descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds from this sorted list.

Table 2:

Medium-Term Return on NAV (last three years):

We then sort our list on a three-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and select the top 10 funds. Please note that the huge drawdowns in the case of MLPs during the pandemic (caused by the Oil crash) and subsequent recovery have led to too many MLPs and energy funds topping this list (3-year NAV gains). For this reason, we make a shortlist based on the total score and keep only a few of them on this list.

Table 3:

Five-Year Annualized Return on NAV:

We then sorted our list on the five-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 4:

Coverage Ratio (Distributions Vs. Earnings):

The coverage ratio is derived by dividing the earnings per share by the distribution amount for a specific period. Please note that in some cases, the coverage ratio may be a bit inaccurate since the "earnings per share" may be three to six months old. But in most cases, it's fairly accurate. Furthermore, the coverage ratio is not critical for pure equity funds as they generate very little dividend income, and they cover most of their distributions from capital gains. However, we sorted our list on coverage ratio and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 5:

Excess Return Over Distribution:

If a fund provides a very high distribution but low returns over 3, 5, or 10-year periods, it does not help the long-term interests of the investor. In simple terms, the fund is just overpaying the distributions and hurting the NAV in the long term. That is why we calculate a factor we like to call "excess return." This excess return is what is provided by the fund over and above the distribution rate over a specified time period. We calculate it by subtracting the distribution rate from the average of the three-year and five-year NAV returns. In this list, we included the top 10 funds.

Table 6:

Total Weight (Quality Score) Calculated Up to This Point:

Note: The Total Weight calculation is not fully completed at this point since we have not considered the 10-year NAV return. However, we select the top 15 names based on current calculations.

Table 7:

From the selections so far, we have 75 funds in total (tables 1-7).

We will need to see if there are any duplicates among them. In our current list of 75 funds, there were 26 duplicates, meaning there are funds that appeared more than once. The following names appear twice (or more):

Appeared twice: CSQ, EFR, EIC, EOD, EOS, IIF, MSD, MXF, PEO, SCD (10 duplicates).

Appeared three times: EMO, KYN, XFLT (6 duplicates).

Appeared four times: PDX, STK (6 duplicates).

Appeared five times: TWN (4 duplicates).

Once we remove 23 duplicate rows, we are left with 49 (75- 26) funds.

Note: It may be worthwhile to mention here that just because a fund has appeared multiple times does not necessarily make it an attractive candidate. Sometimes, a fund may appear multiple times simply for the wrong reasons, like a high current discount, a high excess discount, or a very high distribution rate that may not be sustainable. However, during the second filtering stage, it may not score well on the overall quality score due to other factors like a poor track record. That said, if a fund has appeared four times or more, it may be worth a second look.

Narrowing Down to Just 10 Funds

In our list of funds, we may already have some of the best probable candidates. However, so far, they have been selected based on one (or two) criteria that each may be good at. That's not nearly enough. So, we will apply a combination of criteria by applying weights to eight factors to calculate the total quality score and filter out the best ones.

We will apply weights to each of the eight criteria:
