5-YR NAV return

5.0

10-YR NAV return or 10-YR Total return

5.0

If less than ten years of history, an average of three and five-year returns is taken.

Excess NAV return over distribution rate

5.0

Adjusted Coverage Ratio

7.5

This factor is adjusted for certain factors. The adjustment is performed based on the type of fund, such as equity and sector funds.

Once we have calculated the weights, we combine them to calculate the "Total Combined Weight," also called the "Quality Score."

The sorted list (spreadsheet) of 49 funds on the "combined total weight or quality score" is attached here.

File-for-export_-_10_Best_CEFs_-_Aug_2025.xlsx

10-Positions Portfolio of The Month

Before we get to the final five, we will narrow down our list to 20 names. So, we sorted our table based on the sector and then the quality score. Furthermore, in this round of selections, we mostly pick one or two top funds from each sector (based on quality score). There is a bit of subjective judgment, as we tend to prioritize funds that pay regular and consistent distributions on a monthly or quarterly basis. We also weed out any fund that may have a very poor track record in terms of NAV performance. Funds that may have inconsistent dividends (even if they are high) generally do not make it to our top list. Furthermore, be aware that many times, single-country funds score high in our rankings, but most of them pay variable dividends on an annual or semi-annual basis. In addition, being single-country funds, they can be inherently riskier since their future returns are tied to just one country, be it economic, regulatory, or geopolitical factors. For these reasons, they generally do not make it to our top 5 lists, but they do appear on our list of 20. Individual investors could consider them based on their goals.

Here's the list of the top 20 selections (from different asset classes) in the order of sector:

Table 8:

10-Best-CEFs of the Month:

So far, we have followed our filtering criteria to a large extent. However, in the end, if we only want to select ten funds, we will need to apply our subjective judgment to some degree. While we narrow down this list to ten names, we would be mindful to keep the list as diversified as possible in terms of sectors and asset classes. Since this step is somewhat subjective, the choice would differ from person to person. Nonetheless, here are the selections for this month, based on our perspective (in order of Quality Score):

Here's the list of the top 10 selections this month (in order of sector):

(STK), (MXF), (SCD), (EOD), (KYN), (BANX), (PEO), (XFLT), (NIE), and (ADX).

Table-9:

Some information about the selections:

We will only briefly describe the selected funds. Since there are 10 of them, we will not be providing a very detailed analysis of them.

Here are some details on our 10 selections:

(STK) Columbia Seligman Prem Technology Growth Fund:

The fund invests in a portfolio of high-growth technology stocks with an option overlay strategy designed to mitigate downside volatility and generate income. The fund writes call options on the NASDAQ 100 Index or its exchange-traded fund equivalent on a month-to-month basis. The aggregate notional amount of the call options could typically range between 40% and 50%. STK's yield is at best moderate at 5.80% for a closed-end fund, but the total returns are highly impressive, even beating many pure-play growth funds.

(MXF) Mexico Fund:

As the name suggests, this is a Mexico country-specific fund and generally invests above 95% of its assets in Mexican securities (or listed on Mexican exchanges). It is primarily an equity fund with over 95% allocation to equities, with the rest in fixed income. The current yield is moderate at 5.5% but the discount is roughly -12%. However, the discount has narrowed somewhat in recent times. It pays distributions on a quarterly basis and raised them by over 13% last month. We recommend not being overweight in any of the country-specific funds.

(SCD) LMP Capital and Income

SCD is a fairly diversified fund invested in equity as well as fixed-income securities from many sectors. However, it has its largest positions in MLPs and technology companies. Besides these two, it has allocations, generally under 10%, to many other sectors like REITs, Financials, and Healthcare.

The fund is categorized under "Global Allocation," but nearly 85% is invested in U.S. securities. Broadly, the current allocation is as follows:

  • 60% Common Stocks, mostly US-based.
  • 15% Energy MLPs
  • 12% REITs equity
  • 12% Preferred and Bond securities

(EOD) Allspring Global Dividend Opportunity Fund:

The fund's primary investment ​goal is to seek a high level of current income, with a secondary goal of long-term growth of capital. The fund is invested roughly 80% in global equities with a focus on dividends, and the rest in fixed income (18%) and cash equivalents (2%). The equity portion is invested in 70-80 companies spread across sectors and regions. The fund also employs an options strategy to enhance income and to generate gains on call options. The fund also uses a modest leverage of roughly 15%.

The allocation to the U.S. is roughly two-thirds of the total at 67% and the rest is invested in countries such as the U.K., France, Japan, China, Germany, Italy, Canada, Taiwan, and Brazil. The largest sector is information Technology at 28%, followed by Financials at 18% and Communications at 11%.

(KYN) Kayne Anderson Energy Infrastructure Fund:

It is one of the popular MLP funds invested in the midstream companies. The MLP funds have had an incredible run in recent times. They are showing very high returns on a 3-year and 5-year basis due to the energy price crash during the 2020 Covid period. However, they do not score very well on a 10-year return basis. That being said, they are also scoring well in our rankings because of attractive yields and discounts. Currently, KYN is yielding close to 8% and offers nearly a 10% discount. One could also look at another MLP, NB Energy Infra and Income Fund (NML).

(BANX) ArrowMark Financial Corp:

The fund invests in preferred equity, subordinated debt, convertible securities, regulatory capital relief securities, and, to a lesser extent, common equity. The primary objective is to provide a steady, high income and, more or less, a stable NAV.

The fund offers a well-covered distribution that is reasonably high at 8.75% and is fully supported by the NII (net investment income) while maintaining a stable (or growing) NAV. Secondly, it offers exposure to floating rate instruments that have benefited from the rise in interest rates, which may appear to be negative right now. But rates may not come down fast enough. Also, the fund did reasonably well even during the previous zero-interest rate environment, and the management appears to be confident that it can maintain the NII. However, it may be noted that while the fund raised the distribution early this year, it has now lowered it to the same level as in 2024.

(PEO) Adams Natural Resources Fund:

PEO is a great fund to own the major oil companies at a discount (currently -10%). This fund provides very nice and diversified exposure to large energy companies. However, the fund is somewhat concentrated at the top, given the fact that the two oil majors, Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX), together represent nearly 37% of the total assets. In addition, it currently provides an attractive yield of close to 10%.

PEO has performed consistently well in our ratings, though the fund prices go up or down based on the oil prices. Please keep in mind that this is an energy sector-specific fund, and hence, one should allocate accordingly.

(XFLT) XAI Octagon FR & Alternative Inc Trust:

This fund is a bit of a controversial choice. The fund is for investors who truly seek very high income. As it is often said, the higher the income yields, the higher the risk. So, please use due diligence. In a portfolio of 10 funds, as a whole, the risk may be contained. Moreover, this fund may balance out our other names that are all heavy on the equity side. The income yield is obviously very attractive at more than 15%. This fund can be used as a means of diversification and elevating the income yield for the overall CEF-based portfolio.

This fund is somewhat complex to understand, but this is how the fund describes itself: "The Trust invests in a dynamically managed portfolio of floating-rate credit instruments and other structured credit investments within the private markets. Under normal market conditions, the Trust will invest at least 80% of managed assets in senior secured loans, CLO debt, and CLO equity."

The fund is currently invested to the extent of 46% in Senior First-Lien Loans, which are considered relatively safer. However, it also invests nearly 50% of its assets in CLOs (Collateralized Loan Obligations) and nearly 3/4th of those in CLO equity tranches, which are considered the riskier part of CLOs. Its closest competitor fund in its space is Eagle Point Credit (ECC).

It is important to note that the fund has cut down the monthly distribution amount twice since December last year. That is also part of the reason that the fund's premium has turned into a sizable discount.

(NIE) Virtus Equity & Convertible Income Fund:

This fund is a hybrid fund with nearly 60% equity, 30% convertibles, and the rest in bonds and cash. With the kind of asset mix, the fund results in lower volatility than the S&P500, but also slightly lower performance than the S&P500 over the long period of time.

The fund offers an attractive yield of 8.2% but also offers a discount of almost -10%. Also, this fund does not use any leverage, which is an advantage in the current high-interest-rate environment.

(ADX) Adams Diversified Equity Fund:

This fund regularly appears in our selections under the large-cap U.S. equity category. We feel, under the category of large-cap core dividend funds, this is one of the best funds. The fund has a very long and excellent performance history. The past performance is excellent at 16.8%, 15.1%, and 12.9% on an annualized basis ("NAV") for the past 3, 5, and 10-year periods, respectively. Another plus with ADX is that it uses zero leverage and charges much lower fees compared to its peers.

Sure, there are other funds that are quite attractive, such as the Special Opportunities Fund (SPE) and Liberty All-Star Equity (USA). However, ADX outshines them on a long-term basis.

Some of the top holdings of the fund are similar to those of the S&P500, with many of the Magnificent Seven among them, such as Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), Meta (META), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Visa (V), and Broadcom (AVGO).

CEF-Specific Investment Risks

It goes without saying that CEFs generally have some additional risks. This section is specifically relevant for investors who are new to CEF investing, but in general, all CEF investors should be aware of it. For more details on these specific risks, please read our blog post on CEFs.

  • Use of high leverage and high fees
  • High volatility (compared to the broader market)
  • Premium over NAVs (market price higher than the NAV)
  • Asset-specific concentration risk.

Concluding Thoughts

The broader market, as represented by the S&P500, provides a dismal yield of less than 1.20%. Many income investors and retirees can't survive on that kind of income. For income investors, we recommend building a core bucket of DGI (Dividend growth) stocks and supplementing it with a basket of CEFs to increase the overall income.

We use our screening process to highlight the best closed-end funds for investment each month. Our filtering process demands that our selections have an above-average long-term performance record, offer an average of roughly 8% distributions, and are relatively cheaper with reasonable discounts. We have also provided a larger list of ten CEFs from many different asset classes.

This month, as a group, the selected 10 best funds are offering an average distribution rate of just under 9% (as of 08/15/2025). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had an excellent record over the long term and collectively returned 13.7% and 16.3% (annualized) in the last three and five years, respectively. The average discount (to NAV) is good, at nearly -8%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Please note that these selections are based on our proprietary rating system and are dynamic in nature. So, they can change from month to month (or even week to week). At the same time, some funds can repeat from one month to the next if they remain attractive over an extended period. Furthermore, note that if a fund has not made it to our list, it does not mean it is not good. It could be because they may not be attractively priced at this time or trading at a premium while running our filtering process.

data.title: Weekly Indicators: Consumer Spending Remains Strong
data.description:
Purpose

I look at the high-frequency weekly indicators because, while they can be very noisy, they provide a good nowcast of the economy and will telegraph the maintenance or change in the economy well before monthly or quarterly data is available. They are also an excellent way to "mark your beliefs to market". In general, I go in order of long leading indicators, then short leading indicators, then coincident indicators.

A Note on Methodology

Data is presented in a "just the facts, ma'am" format with a minimum of commentary so that bias is minimized.

Where relevant, I include 12-month highs and lows in the data in parentheses to the right. All data taken from St. Louis FRED unless otherwise linked.

A few items (e.g., Financial Conditions indexes, regional Fed indexes, stock prices, the yield curve) have their own metrics based on long-term studies of their behavior.

Where data is seasonally adjusted, generally it is scored positively if it is within the top 1/3 of that range, negative in the bottom 1/3, and neutral in between. Where it is not seasonally adjusted and there are seasonal issues, waiting for the YoY change to change sign will lag the turning point. Thus, I make use of a convention: data is scored neutral if it is less than 1/2 as positive/negative as at its 12-month extreme.

With long leading indicators, which by definition turn at least 12 months before a turning point in the economy as a whole, there is an additional rule: data is automatically negative if, during an expansion, it has not made a new peak in the past year, with the sole exception that it is scored neutral if it is moving in the right direction and is close to making a new high.

For all series where a graph is available, I have provided a link to where the relevant graph can be found.

Recap of Monthly Reports

July data included another decline in housing permits, with a noisy advance in starts. Partly as a result, the index of Leading Indicators declined slightly again. Existing home sales remained rangebound, improving slightly, while their prices are on the verge of declining YoY.

Long leading indicators

Interest rates and credit spreads

Rates

  • BAA corporate bond index 6.05%, up +0.05% w/w (1-yr range: 5.33-6.43)
  • 10-year Treasury bonds 4.26%, down -0.06% w/w (3.66-4.98)
  • Credit spread 1.79%, up +0.11% w/w (1.36-2.02)

(Graph at Moody's Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield | FRED | St. Louis Fed )

Yield curve

  • 10-year minus 2-year: +0.57%, unchanged w/w (-1.07 - +0.53)
  • 10-year minus 3-month: +0.06%, down -0.04% w/w (-1.65 - 0.17)
  • 2-year minus Fed funds: -0.74%, down -0.16% w/w

(Graph at 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Minus 2-Year Treasury Constant Maturity | FRED | St. Louis Fed )

30-Year conventional mortgage rate (from Mortgage News Daily)

  • 6.62%, up +0.06% w/w (6.11-7.26)

Treasury and corporate bond yields, as well as mortgage rates, remain lower than their near 12-month highs of a number of months ago. The spreads between Treasuries and investment-grade and high-yield bonds continue to show little change, having meandered in a relatively narrow range for most of the past several years.

The yield curve measures remain where they have been, more or less, for months, with the 10-2-year spread being the most positive and the 2-year vs. Fed funds being the most negative. The 10-year minus 3-month remained neutral this week.

Housing

Mortgage applications (from the Mortgage Bankers Association)

  • Purchase apps up +0.1% at 149 (114-163) [SA]
  • Purchase apps 4 wk avg. down -1 to 147 [SA]
  • Purchase apps YoY% up +23% [NSA]
  • Purchase apps YoY 4 wk avg. +14% [NSA]
  • Refi apps down -3% w/w [SA]
  • Refi apps YoY up +23% [SA]

*[SA] = seasonally adjusted, [NSA] = not seasonally adjusted

Real Estate Loans (from the FRB)

  • Up +0.1% w/w
  • Up +1.3% YoY (tied for multi-year low)

(Graph at Real Estate Loans, All Commercial Banks | FRED | St. Louis Fed )

Both purchase mortgage and refinancing applications have been volatile, but within a limited range over the past 18 months. Both are now substantially higher YoY but remain close to their longer-term bottoms, so they score neutral.

In 2023, real estate loans sank below 1/2 of their 12-month high, the last housing indicator to turn negative, and have generally continued to worsen, and tied their post-pandemic low once again this week.

Money supply

The Federal Reserve has discontinued this weekly series. Data is now only released monthly. June data was just released four weeks ago:

  • M1 m/m up +0.6%, YoY Real M1 up +1.6% (3-year high) (worst: -1.4% 8/23)
  • M2 m/m up +0.6%, YoY Real M2 up +1.9% (2.1% June 2025 3-year high) (worst -8.6% 9/23)

No recession has happened without a YoY real M1 negative, or YoY real M2 below +2.5%. Real M2 fell below that threshold in March 2022. Real M1 also turned negative as of May 2022. In the past, when real money supply has rebounded to the levels it is at now, it was either during the latter part of a recession or in the period of recovery, so both of these are positive long leading metrics now.

Corporate profits (56% actual + 44% estimated) from I/B/E/S via FactSet at p. 29) (no new report until August 29)

  • Q2 up +1.42 to 66.39, up +4.3% q/q

FactSet estimates earnings, which are replaced by actual earnings as they are reported, and are updated weekly. The "neutral" band is +/-3%. I also average the previous two quarters together until at least 100 companies have actually reported. In Q1, earnings wound up lower by more than -3%, which turned the rating negative. But in Q2, earnings have rebounded sharply, and as of two weeks ago, the rating has changed from neutral to positive.

Credit conditions (from the Chicago Fed)

  • Financial Conditions Index down -.02 (looser) to -0.56 (-0.42 – -0.67)
  • Adjusted Index (removing background economic conditions) down -0.01 (looser) to -0.53 (-0.34 - -0.61)
  • Leverage subindex up +0.03 (less loose) to -0.44 (+0.13 - -0.76)

In these indexes, lower = better for the economy. The Chicago Fed's Adjusted Index's real break-even point is roughly -0.25. In the leverage index, a negative number is good, and a positive number is poor. The historical breakeven point has been -0.5 for the unadjusted Index. After briefly deteriorating to neutral several months ago, all three were recently solidly positive, but the unadjusted index remains neutral.

Short leading indicators

Economic Indicators from the late Jeff Miller’s “Weighing the Week Ahead”

  • Miller Score (formerly "C-Score"): up +1 to 396, down -25 m/m (232 8/30/24 - 421 7/25/25) Components: Yield curve 53 Phillips curve 343
  • St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index: down -0.2479 to -0.8153 (+0.5569 4/11/25 – -0.9773 1/24/25)
  • BCIp from Georg Vrba: up +3.5 to +46.0 as of 7/30/25 iM's Business Cycle Index (100 is max value, below 25 is recession signal averaging 20 weeks ahead)

The Miller Score is designed to look 52 weeks ahead for whether or not a recession is possible. Any score over 500 means no recession. This number fell below that threshold at the beginning of August 2021, so not only is it negative, but we are now years into the "recession-eligible" time period. This indicator is heavily influenced by both the yield curve and the still very low unemployment rate vs. gas prices (expecting a reversal), which explains why it remains negative. My colleague who compiles this score began disaggregating the two components several months ago.

The St. Louis Financial Stress Index is one where a negative score is a positive for the economy and during its limited existence, has risen above zero before a recession by less than one year. It remained very positive until briefly in April. It then recovered and remains among its better post-pandemic readings.

For well over a year, IM has stated that its system does not forecast a recession, although in the last two months, it has edged closer to its threshold.

Trade-weighted US$

  • Unchanged at 120.76 w/w, down -2.0% YoY (last week) (broad) (118.76 – 129.67) (Graph at Nominal Broad U.S. Dollar Index
  • Down -0.12 to 97.74 w/w, down -3.0% YoY (major currencies) (graph at link) (99.01 – 109.66)

The neutral range for the US$ is between unchanged and 5% higher YoY. At 5% or more YoY, the strength suppresses exports and incentivizes imports, a negative for the economy. The trend in these measures had been from below 0 (thus positive) to neutral, to above 5% (negative) for 18 months, until the beginning of this year. Both measures are lower YoY now, so are the positives.

A reminder that I mechanically score a slowly depreciating US dollar as a positive because it makes exports cheaper and imports more expensive, thereby assisting domestic production.

Commodity prices

Bloomberg Commodity Index

  • Up +1.25 to 101.63 (93.30 - 107.71)
  • Up +5.4% YoY

(Graph at https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/bcom?countrycode=xx )

Bloomberg Industrial Metals ETF (from Bloomberg) (graph at link)

  • 141.54, up +0.09 w/w (130.47 -173.08 )
  • Down -3.7% YoY

Beginning four months ago, the industrial measure gradually weakened from positive to negative. Then the broad commodity index declined sharply into negative territory, but then rebounded into the neutral range. By contrast, the industrial metals measures remained very negative. This can be attributed to whether or not oil is included among the commodities tracked. Last week the industrial measure turned back higher YoY, and was so positive. Please note, importantly, that if commodities were not priced in US$ terms but in other major currencies, it is a completely different story. In particular, that industrial commodities are weaker YoY, despite a weaker US$, is definitely not a sign of global strength.

Stock prices: S&P 500 (from CNBC) (graph at link)

  • Up 0.3% to 6466.91

In July and again this month, stocks have made several new all-time highs. As a result, this indicator is positive.

Regional Fed New Order Indexes

(*indicates report this week)

  • Empire State, up +13.4 to 15.4
  • *Philly down -20.5 to -1.9
  • Richmond down -13 to -25
  • Kansas City up +13 to +2
  • Dallas up +3.7 to -3.6
  • Month-over-month rolling average: down -4 to -2

The regional average is more volatile than the ISM manufacturing index but usually correctly forecasts its month-over-month direction. Since spring 2022, these have gradually declined to neutral and then negative. In the past 45 days, these have improved to neutral, and if the average increases just +1 more to +3, this will score as a positive.

In April, I wrote that “I am strongly considering adding the regional service indexes this month, to see if the consumer segment of the economy suddenly shrinks in as close to real time as reasonably available.” Those reports are posted below, with new reports posted in the past week noted with an *:

  • *Empire State down -2.4 to -11.7
  • *Philly down -30.2 to -0.3
  • Richmond up +6 to -8
  • Kansas City down -5 to -2
  • Dallas up +6.4 to +2.0
  • Average: down -6 to -4

The regional non-manufacturing reports have stabilized at negative levels since May. Meanwhile, the ISM non-manufacturing index, averaged over three months, has continued to weaken. In their latest report released two weeks ago, new orders were negative, and the headline index is only 0.1 above its equipoise point. This means the economically weighted ISM indexes combined showed contraction.

Employment metrics

Initial jobless claims

  • 235,000, up +11,000 w/w, up +1.3% YoY
  • 4-week average 226,250, up 4,500 w/w, down -3.9% YoY

(Graph at St. Louis FRED)

Jobless claims were positive for most of 2024, except for hurricane-influenced weeks during the autumn. They subsequently returned to neutral, then briefly positive, before turning back to neutral. We have had very good reports for the last seven weeks until this week, and four weeks ago, this indicator improved to positive. This appears to be an issue with unresolved seasonality accounting for educational layoffs and rehiring, but it could also be a reflection of non-native born workers in certain industries being deported or being afraid to show up for work, meaning remaining crews are much more tightly held.

Temporary staffing index (from the American Staffing Association) (graph at link)

  • Up +1 to 88 w/w
  • Down -1.0% YoY (low -13.6%- high -1.5% 8/7/25)

This series has been negative since early 2023. YoY comparisons continued to be negative throughout 2024. It remains frankly recessionary. I suspect this has been a secular change, giving a false signal as a result. Relatively speaking, the YoY reading has been trending “less bad” for the past number of months, and finally, since it is now more than half the way to unchanged on a YoY basis, to neutral as of earlier this year.

Tax Withholding (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • $264.2 B for the last 20 reporting days vs. $248.0 B last year, up +$16.2 B or +6.5%

These were very positive for almost all of 2024, and with the exception of seven weeks (four after an April 15 anomaly), the trend has continued this year.

Oil prices and usage (from the E.I.A.)

  • Oil up +$0.67 to $63.79 w/w, down -7.0% YoY ($58.21 - $83.88)
  • Gas prices up +.01 to $3.13 w/w, down -$0.24 YoY
  • Usage 4-week average down -1.2% YoY

(Information can be found at “This Week In Petroleum” at the E.I.A.’s website)

Oil prices first declined into the bottom 1/3rd of their 3-year range, and then turned positive late in 2024, and then declined even further earlier this year. After the Israel-Iran hostilities last month, oil prices increased but have since receded back to close to four-year lows.

Gas prices also recently made new 3-year lows, and so are also positive. Last year, the mileage driven had been generally negative. It turned neutral and then positive earlier this year, but negative again last week. Since March of this year, it has oscillated between positive and negative.

Note: given this measure’s extreme volatility, I believe the best measure is against their 3-year average.

Bank lending rates

  • 4.32% Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR), down -0.02% w/w

The TED Spread has been discontinued, and as of last September, so was LIBOR. At the suggestion of a reader, I began to track the SOFR instead. Unfortunately, SOFR has only been in existence since 2018, so there is no track record for how it might behave around normal recessions (vs. the pandemic). Over the past 5 years, it does appear to have matched the trend in LIBOR. Given the alternatives, I am now including it in my list of indicators in the conclusion.

Coincident indicators

Tariff payments (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • July 2025 vs. July 2024: $29.6 B vs. $8.7 B (+$20.9 B)
  • August 1 - 20 2025 vs. August 1 - 20 2024: $6.5 B vs. $2.1 B (+$4.4 B)

I have inaugurated this measure temporarily since other sources, such as inferences from the CPI and PPI, are imprecise and speculative so far. The above information comes directly from the Daily Treasury Statement. These fees are either absorbed by the importer or passed on to the consumer, or some proportion of each, and are removed from potential consumption on other items. Note that the above payments include very little, if anything, from the further tariff hikes that went into effect two weeks ago.

St. Louis FRED Weekly Economic Index

  • Up +0.04 to 2.54 w/w (Low 1.65 November 2, 2024 - high 3.21 November 23, 2024)

This measure was in the neutral range during most of early 2024 but generally positive (above 2.00) since then and came very close to setting a new 12-month high three months ago, before declining. After four weeks back in the neutral range, including a new 6-month low, nine weeks ago, it returned to positive territory.

Restaurant reservations YoY (from Open Table) State of the Restaurant Industry |

  • Last week: 7-day average up +10% YoY, vs. +10% one week prior

Since 2023, this index has been very volatile, swinging between positive, neutral, and negative several times. In general, earlier this year, the YoY trends improved, but with a great deal of noise. In the past two months, however, this metric trended sharply higher before backing off somewhat last week.

Consumer spending

  • Johnson Redbook up +5.9% YoY, 4-week average +5.8% (high 7.4% 4/25/25; 4.8% 2/1 and 7/3, 2025) United States Redbook Index

The Redbook index has been positive since February last year. It had very good readings in April and early May, probably due to consumers front-running anticipated tariff price increases and shortages. Since then, it had been weakening—until five weeks ago, when it stabilized. Two weeks ago, it increased sharply and has remained very positive since.

Consumer inflation by Truflation (Independent, economic & financial data in real time on-chain | Truflation)

  • Up +0.04% to +2.11% YoY (High 3.11% 12/11/24 - Low 1.06% 9/13/24)

This recent addition is a daily update to inflation, similar to the “billion prices project” of the last decade (which required a subscription). I have not added this to my list below of coincident or leading indicators, but needless to say, it is an up-to-the-moment reading on this very important indicator. This series has been volatile, and the YoY comparisons have been turning higher since September but have been very low in the last several months, although it did make another new two-month high twelve weeks ago.

Real Consumer Spending

  • Up +3.8% YoY (high - 6.0% 4/25/25; 12-month low 1.7%% 12/16/24)

This metric premiered at the beginning of last year. One of my most important mantras is that consumption leads to employment. Real retail sales have a long history of doing so, but are only reported on a monthly basis.

The weekly result is derived simply by subtracting YoY inflation as measured by Truflation from the YoY change in nominal consumer spending as measured by Redbook. While it will be somewhat noisy, it should anticipate changes in the monthly measures ahead of time. It backed off from the 12-month high it set in February and then gradually rebounded and made another 12-month high several months ago. After mainly weeks in the upper part of its 12-month range, it was tepid at the beginning of the year before improving in the past two months, even making a new 12-month high before backing off. With nominal spending relatively low and inflation holding generally steady, this indicator weakened, although still positive. It has improved considerably along with nominal consumer spending in the past several months.

Transport

Railroads (from the AAR)

  • Carloads down -0.9% YoY (cumulative 2025 vs. 2024 up +2.7%)
  • Intermodal units down -0.6% YoY (cumulative up +4.4%)
  • Total loads down -0.7% YoY (cumulative up +3.6%)

(Graph at Railfax Report - North American Rail Freight Traffic Carloading Report )

Shipping transport

  • Harpex up +18 to 2210 (new 24-month high)
  • Baltic Dry Index down -146 to 1893

Rail was mixed for many weeks before turning positive in March, presumably reflecting businesses attempting to get inventory before the new tariffs kick in. Intermodal rail loads had steadily decelerated since then, and five weeks ago turned negative. The total number of loads was negative for several weeks as well. All three measures improved sharply in July, and remained positive—until this week.

Harpex was as high as nearly 4500 almost 2 years ago but declined as low as 810 in December of last year before rising, setting repeated 12-month highs in the past four months. The usual interpretation for this is that demand is straining against supply, showing a strong global economy and thus very positive.

Similarly, the BDI was in a generally declining trend throughout 2022 before bottoming at a three-year low of 530 in February 2023. The overall trend has been higher since then, rising to a high of 3346 last December, probably reflecting the Houthi attacks on shipping in the Red Sea. This year, its low was 1309 in January, and its recent high was 2419 last March. It declined to the bottom 1/3rd of its one-year range during the rest of the year but rebounded all the way to the top of its six-month range earlier this year before retreating back into the middle of that range.

I am wary of reading too much into price indexes like this, since they are heavily influenced by supply (as in, a huge overbuilding of ships in the last decade) as well as demand.

Steel production ( American Iron and Steel Institute)

  • Down -1.4% w/w
  • Up +2.8% YoY

This metric has been very volatile for over a year. Recently, it improved to neutral and then positive for two weeks before backing off to neutral again in May. Last week, its YoY measure was sufficiently small to change the rating to neutral, and five weeks ago it returned to positive.

Summary And Conclusion

Below are this week’s spreadsheets of the long leading, short leading, and coincident readings. Check marks indicate the present reading. If there has been a change this week, the prior reading is marked with an X:

.

There were a few changes to any of the indicators this week. The long leading indicators continue to be very mixed, while the short leading and coincident indicators remain very positive this week, despite several negative component changes.

To reiterate a comment I made last week, a number of the short-leading and coincident indicators are tied to the value of the US$, including commodities and shipping prices. Normally, prices are mainly rated based on demand, so an increase in price means an increase in economic activity.

I continue to believe that consumer spending and initial jobless claims are the two most important high-frequency indicators at present, and both remain well behaved.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Wall Street this week was headed for a solid loss, weighed down by a rout in technology stocks and disappointing retail earnings. However, a dovish signal from Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell on Friday at the annual Jackson Hole Economic Policy Symposium sparked a big rally, helping the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) eke out gains for the week.

While the S&P and also the blue-chip Dow (DJI) notched weekly gains, the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) posted a decline, as cautious investors ahead of Jackson Hole sold off pricey megacap technology stocks. Meanwhile, retail giants Walmart (WMT) and Target (TGT) slid after their quarterly results, with the latter also naming a new chief executive.

Anticipation ahead of Powell's speech was high, with market participants hoping for comments on easing monetary policy. Though Powell did not fully commit to cutting interest rates, he noted that the downside risks to the labor market were increasing and that the "baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance."

For the week, the S&P (SP500) added +0.3%, while the Dow (DJI) gained +1.5%. The Nasdaq (COMP:IND) slipped -0.6%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.
